metadata.title: The Vending Machine Problem
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. An AI that just dispenses answers is a vending machine, and vending machines don't teach. Socratic prompting instead makes models probe each other's assumptions and demand evidence. That distinction between dispensing and probing matters more than it first appears. Models so good at solving problems can leave students never learning to solve them themselves.

artifactLines: An AI that just dispenses answers is a vending machine, and vending machines don't teach.
Socratic prompting instead makes models probe each other's assumptions and demand evidence.
That distinction between dispensing and probing matters more than it first appears.
Models so good at solving problems can leave students never learning to solve them themselves.

beats.narration_text: The vending-machine metaphor landed hard: a model that dispenses answers but never teaches. I want to build the Socratic alternative instead. Show me: (1) why models being so good at solving problems means students never learn to solve them themselves, (2) how to prompt two models to probe each other's assumptions and demand evidence rather than hand over answers, and (3) a concrete Socratic prompt template I could paste in so the AI interrogates my reasoning instead of finishing my thinking for me.

props.command: The vending-machine metaphor landed hard: a model that dispenses answers but never teaches. I want to build the Socratic alternative instead. Show me: (1) why models being so good at solving problems means students never learn to solve them themselves, (2) how to prompt two models to probe each other's assumptions and demand evidence rather than hand over answers, and (3) a concrete Socratic prompt template I could paste in so the AI interrogates my reasoning instead of finishing my thinking for me.
output: You’ve asked your AI tutor a simple algebra question
They are vending machines for information

beats.narration_text: This is not a malfunction. But vending machines don’t teach.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is not a malfunction. But vending machines don’t teach.

beats.narration_text: They probe each other’s assumptions. They demand evidence for claims.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: They probe each other’s assumptions. They demand evidence for claims.

beats.narration_text: The distinction matters more than you might think. The models were so good at solving problems that students never learned to solve them themselves.

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — build

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'The Vending Machine Problem' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each.… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.

props.command: Take the central idea from 'The Vending Machine Problem' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the failure mode I'm most likely to hit.
